results.summary: The Board found that there was no bad faith established in this case. There was a commercial logic for filing the contested mark and there was no evidence that the EUTM proprietor or its predecessor had dishonest intentions at the relevant date such as hindering the cancellation applicants' business activity. Furthermore, the company filing for the contested mark was under no obligation to inform the cancellation applicants nor to ask their authorisation for the filing of the contested mark. The other circumstances put forward by the cancellation applicants also could not demonstrate any bad faith in the sense of Article 51(1)(b) CTMR (now Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR). The Board of Appeal dismissed the appeal.

results.quotation: (06/12/2024, R 0012/2024‑4, SHAPE OF A TETRA BRIK (3D))
results.summary: Cancellation request based on Article 7(1)(e)(ii) CTMR and Article 51(1)(b) CTMR (now Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR). A PCT application, including its drawings, illustrates that a prismatic shape such as the contested reduces the amount of liquid packaging paperboard needed to hold a given volume compared to traditional parallelepiped shapes. Secondary advantages obtained are that shapes, including the contested shape, applying the invention remain compatible with package stability and handling properties. Not all results of a technical nature are ‘technical results’ within the meaning of Article 7(1)(e)(ii) CTMR. In this case, Article 7(1)(e)(ii) CTMR does not apply because the shape at issue has no impact on the utility that the containers in question are intended to have. The result of improving the volume-used material ratio relates to the manufacturing process of the product itself, not to its use by the relevant professional public of intermediaries purchasing empty containers to fill them with liquids and foodstuffs.

keywords: Classification of the goods and services
results.summary:
The registration of a mark for goods in an incorrect class does not in itself demonstrate a dishonest intention. In any event, the incorrect classification was not such as to confer on the EUTM applicant any advantage over its competitors (§ 38-41).

Bad faith cannot be presumed on the basis of the mere finding that the EUTM applicant had no economic activity corresponding to the goods and services at the date of filing of the contested mark. The fact that the contested mark was revoked for the goods and services in Classes 29 and 35 for lack of genuine use does not mean that the proprietor acted in bad faith when it applied for registration of that mark, in the absence of other relevant and consistent evidence. Revocation proceedings are the appropriate proceedings for reducing the scope of protection conferred on a trade mark where there is no genuine use (§ 43-46).

keywords: Classification of the goods and services
results.summary:
The registration of a mark for goods in an incorrect class does not in itself demonstrate a dishonest intention. In any event, the incorrect classification was not such as to confer on the EUTM applicant any advantage over its competitors (§ 38-41).

Bad faith cannot be presumed on the basis of the mere finding that the EUTM applicant had no economic activity corresponding to the goods and services at the date of filing of the contested mark. The fact that the contested mark was revoked for the goods and services in Classes 29 and 35 for lack of genuine use does not mean that the proprietor acted in bad faith when it applied for registration of that mark, in the absence of other relevant and consistent evidence. Revocation proceedings are the appropriate proceedings for reducing the scope of protection conferred on a trade mark where there is no genuine use (§ 43-46).

Corrigendum The EUTM under attack neither falls foul of Article 7(1)(g) of Regulation 40/94 nor was registered in bad faith. The use of the term ‘Swisse’, even if understood as a clear and direct reference to ‘Swiss’ or ‘Switzerland’, which might indicate to customers that the products come from Switzerland, does not – in the abstract – deceive consumers. Since the Board cannot see, how the EUTM could have deceived the public on the date of application, it cannot understand how the EUTM proprietor could have acted in bad faith

The mere use of a sign does not make it possible to conclude that third parties automatically know of that use (§ 30).

Revocation proceedings relating to non-use of the contested mark or its sub-brands may contribute towards showing whether the intentions of the proprietor of the contested mark are honest or dishonest, since the actual use of the mark could serve to prove the trade mark proprietor’s intention of engaging fairly in competition (§ 58).

The EUTM under attack neither falls foul of Article 7(1)(g) of Regulation 40/94 nor was registered in bad faith. The use of the term ‘Swisse’, even if understood as a clear and direct reference to ‘Swiss’ or ‘Switzerland’, which might indicate to customers that the products come from Switzerland, does not – in the abstract – deceive consumers. Since the Board cannot see, how the EUTM could have deceived the public on the date of application, it cannot understand how the EUTM proprietor could have acted in bad faith

According to the cancellation applicant, the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith at the time of the EUTM’s application, namely, 1 April 1996, because it knowingly filed part of the goods in Class 30 (inedible decorations for cakes and pastries) in the wrong class. However, the cancellation applicant has failed to prove that the EUTM proprietor had a subjective motivation, dishonest intention or any other sinister motive at the time of filing the contested EUTM that would depart from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices. Moreover, the EUTM proprietor has sufficiently demonstrated that the filing of the EUTM followed a commercial logic. The presumption of good faith of the contested EUTM is therefore maintained.

results.quotation: (05/01/2016, R 2793/2014‑5, LOVES BABY (fig.) / Baby Love et al.)
results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.nickName: LOVES BABY (fig.) / Baby Love et al.

results.summary: The request for a declaration of invalidity was based on relative grounds (earlier marks) and absolute grounds (bad faith). As to the earlier marks, pursuant to Rule 37 CTMIR, the cancellation applicant must file proof of, inter alia, their existence and his/her entitlement to file the action. In the present case, the cancellation applicant did not substantiate the earlier marks. Pursuant to Rule 39(3) CTMIR, however, the Cancellation Division should have invited her to remedy the deficiencies. Before the Board, the cancellation applicant filed a copy of the renewal certificate of the earlier IR. Although admissible, it does not show her entitlement, since the mark is registered in the name of a company whereas she is a physical person. As to the alleged bad faith, the cancellation applicant claimed that the bad faith on the part of the CTM proprietor stems from the fact that he sought to register the mark in his own name, whereas the earlier mark was registered in the name of a company when both the cancellation applicant and the CTM proprietor where still married. Although it is true that also possible business or contractual relationships between spouses may be relevant for the assessment of bad faith, no documentary evidence as regards to the relationship between the parties was filed, nor regarding  the structure of the company that is the owner of the earlier mark.

keywords: Well known facts need no evidence
results.summary: The decision deals with the question whether genuine use of a CTM for biscuits amounts to use for the general categories 'pastry' or 'confectionery'. The answer of the Board is negative. The terms 'pastry' and 'confectionery' are very broad and cover a wide range of different goods, e.g. cakes, pies, croissants, buns and other viennoiseries (pastry) or chocolate, drops, sweets, marzipan, chewing gums (confectionery). 'Biscuits' form an independent sub-category of 'pastry' and 'confectionery'. It is therefore justified to limit the protection to those goods.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based

results.summary: FACTS:  The invalidity applicant requested the declaration of invalidity of the CTM “Simca” (word) for goods in Class 12 based on bad faith (Article 52 (1) (b) CTMR). The Cancellation Division dismissed the action. The Board of Appeal (BoA) reversed the decision of the first instance and declared the invalidity of the CTM because it found that the application has been filed in bad faith. SUBSTANCE: The General Court (GC) confirmed the Case-Law that the three factors set out in the judgment C-529/07, Chocoladefabriken Lindt & Sprüng are only examples drawn from a number of factors which can be taken into account in order to decide whether the applicant was acting in bad faith at the time of filing the application. Consequently, account may also be taken of the origin of the word or the sign which forms the mark at issue and of the earlier use of that word or sign in business as a mark, in particular by competing undertakings, and of the commercial logic underlying the filing of the application for registration of that word or that sign as a Community trade mark (para. 38 and 39). The mark SIMCA has been used for cars since 1930 and the invalidity applicant has trademark protection for SIMCA in different Member States as France, Germany, Spain etc. for goods in class 12. However, the mark has not been used since 1980 (para. 42). The mark had at the date of filing still a certain degree of reputation among the public interested in cars. The applicant knew this residual reputation of the SIMCA marks and was looking for an appropriate mark which was no longer used and which accordingly had no legal protection (para. 45 - 53). The GC finally concluded that the BoA was entitled to infer from the particular circumstances of the present case that the real purpose of the applicant was to ‘free-ride’ on the reputation of the invalidity applicant’s registered marks and to take advantage of that reputation (para 56). The judgement puts the emphasize that the clear and evident intention to take advantage of the residual reputation of the sign SIMCA on the motor vehicle market, to create an association with the earlier marks and to compete with those earlier marks if they were re-used by the invalidity applicant is to be considered as bad faith in the sense of Article 52 (1) (b) CTMR (para 63). This conclusion is not based on the “offer of compensation” to the invalidity applicant (para. 70). The fact that the applicant had started the use of the registered SIMCA mark is not relevant (para. 74). Also the fact, that the applicant worked in the past for the invalidity applicant is not decisive for the outcome (para. 79).

keywords: Identity of the goods and services

results.summary: The CTM proprietor’s intentions seem to be those argued, namely that filing the CTM was to complete the scope of protection of its trade mark, not just territorially but also as regard to the range of goods. Contrary to one of the notions of bad faith, there is no evidence of any dishonest or unethical conduct but merely the intention to broaden its sign’s scope of protection in order to keep up with market realities, its economic activities and its marks. The appeal is dismissed.

results.quotation: (05/12/2012, R 1766/2011‑2, GALERIA SANDECJA / Galeria et al.)

results.summary: 1. The Office Manual provides that ‘as regards the extension of a time limit, any first request for an extension which is received in time will always be granted independently of the explanation given by the party requesting it. 2. The applicant submitted a request for an extension of time to file evidence on 15 November 2011 i.e. before the deadline of two months starting on 21 September 2011. 3. The Board considers that the principles of good administration require the Office to respect its own commitments. The administration must in principle follow its own instructions as they may give expectations to the public. In the present case, the applicant was entitled to rely on the guidelines laid down in the Manual and published by the Office itself. 4. The case is remitted to the ED for further prosecution and the appeal fee reimbursed.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
EUTM on which IA is based
results.summary:
FACTS: The invalidity applicant sought to cancel CTM No 4363842 BIGAB alleging that its owner was acting in bad faith at the time of filing the CTMA, as its sole objective was to prevent the invalidity applicant from continuing to use its Benelux non-registered mark BIGA for similar products. The CTM was registered on 20 March 2006, but BIGAB has been used in Sweden and other countries since 1991, whereas the invalidity applicant did not start using the sign BIGA (in Benelux countries) until 1996. The Board confirmed the rejection of the request for invalidity. The invalidity applicant filed an action before the General Court.

SUBSTANCE:  The GC followed in essence the argumentation of the Board´s decision and confirmed the jurisprudence in relation to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR (bad faith invalidity ground) as it was interpreted by Case C-529/07 Chocoladefabriken Lindt &Sprüngli [2009] ECR I-4893 (Lindt Goldhase case). The BIGAB Judgment has clarified some other points which have to be taken into account in order to assess whether the applicant of a CTM is acting in bad faith at the time of filing the application.

The GC clarified that the three factors set out in the Lindt Goldhase Judgment that is, i) knowledge by the applicant that a third party is using a sign which could give rise to confusion with the sign for which registration is sought, ii) applicant´s intention of preventing that third party from using such a sign and iii) degree of legal protection enjoyed by the third party´s sign and by the sign for which the registration is sought, were just indicative examples drawn from a number of factors which can be taken into account (Para. 20). In the context of the overall analysis, account may also be taken of i) the origin of the sign (filed for preventing a third party´s sign) and its use since its creation and ii) the commercial logic underlying the registration of that sign as a CTM (Para. 21). 

The fact that the CTM applicant knows or must know that a third party has long been using, in at least one Member State, an identical or similar sign for identical or similar goods, which could give rise to confusion with the sign for which registration is sought, is not sufficient in itself to permit the conclusion that the applicant was acting in bad faith (Para, 27). Moreover, the good faith of an applicant cannot be challenged solely because it is the proprietor of other marks and did not take the initiative to register them as CTMs (Para. 28).
The GC found that it matters little whether or not the goods marketed by the invalidity applicant under the mark BIGA are similar or identical to the goods marketed by the CTM owner, since, in any event, it has not been shown that the sole purpose of the CTM registration was to prevent the invalidity applicant from using the mark BIGA (Para. 32). Similarly, the fact that, a few weeks after successfully registering the mark BIGAB as a CTM, its owner sent a cease and desist letter to the invalidity applicant is not an indication of bad faith, since such a request falls within the scope of the rights attached to a CTM (Article 9 CTMR) (Para. 33). Finally, the fact that the Board of Appeal has relied on the reference in Article 12 CTMR to ‘honest practices in industrial or commercial matters’, for the purposes of defining ‘bad faith’ as used in Article 52(1)(b), was not considered an error in law, even though the subject-matter of the two provisions is different. The GC held that the Board did not err in law by considering the two provisions in parallel, as this was justified by the fact that they are both designed to penalize commercial dishonesty (Para. 35 & 36). 
Conclusion: In essence, the GC held that the intention of the owner of a national mark to protect it ‘on a European scale’ does not constitute a bad faith action against a similar national right designating similar goods, especially when this mark has been previously used in various EU countries and predates the national sign. It matters little in that context that the CTM applicant knew or, at least, should have known, at the time of filing the CTMA, of the use of the other sign which could give rise to confusion with the sign for which registration is sought.

results.quotation: (01/02/2012, T‑291/09, Pollo Tropical CHICKEN ON THE GRILL, EU:T:2012:39)
results.entityName: Pollo Tropical CHICKEN ON THE GRILL
results.nickName: Pollo Tropical CHICKEN ON THE GRILL
results.summary:
On 20 June 1994, the intervener in the case before the GC (the ïntervener¨, CTM owner) filed an application with the OEPM for registration of the following Spanish figurative mark, registered on 20 December 1995
 

On 21 October 1994, the predecessor of the applicant in the case before the GC (the ¨invalidity applicant¨ in the case before OHIM) sought to register with the OEPM the word mark POLLO TROPICAL and a figurative mark, identical to its earlier US mark, for which registration had been applied on 25 April 1994. The applications were refused due to the opposition lodged by the intervener on the basis of the Spanish mark.

On 9 June 2000, the invalidity applicant obtained registration in the United Kingdom of the word mark POLLO TROPICAL and the figurative mark reproduced below for the provision of food and drink under Class 42.
 
On 22 November 2002, the intervener applied for the following CTM for goods and services in Classes 25, 41 and 43.  It was registered on 21 April 2004.
 
On 22 January 2007, the invalidity applicant lodged an application for the declaration of invalidity of the CTM, on the grounds of likelihood of confusion in the UK (based on the earlier UK marks) and bad faith (based, inter alia, on the earlier US mark).

The Cancellation Division rejected the invalidity application.  The applicant failed to adduce proof of genuine use of its UK marks.  Bad faith was not an issue, as there was a continuity or ‘commercial trajectory’ between the Spanish mark and the CTM.  An email correspondence from 2006 in which the intervener fixed a price for the sale of the CTM was not considered conclusive proof of bad faith. 
 
The Board of Appeal dismissed the action of the invalidity applicant.  Bad faith had to be established at the time of the application for a CTM, i.e. in 2002; any bad faith at the time of the application for registration in Spain in 1994 could not be taken into consideration.  No evidence has been adduced showing that the intervener was aware of the applicant’s commercial activities in the US.  The application for a CTM was a normal and foreseeable step in the intervener’s services after it had launched its commercial activity in Spain.  Furthermore, the invalidity applicant did not file an opposition to the CTM application.

Before the General Court the invalidity applicant alleged infringement of Art. 52(1)(b) CTMR, on the ground that the intervener acted in bad faith when the CTM was applied for.  

SUBSTANCE: The General Court dismissed the action. 

The invalidity applicant has not provided any evidence establishing a presumption that the intervener could not be unaware of the existence of the US mark (paragraph 62).  

The well-known character of the US mark has not been established at the time of the application for registration of either the Spanish mark or the CTM.  No document indicates the market share or demonstrates intensity of use and magnitude of investments.  No third-party declaration attesting to the well-known character of the mark was submitted.

The invalidity applicant failed to prove genuine use of its UK mark, identical to the mark at issue.  It is doubtful that the intervener, the owner of a small pizzeria in a village on the Canary Islands, could, through its national mark, block the invalidity applicant’s expansion in the rest of the EU (paragraph 84).  

The invalidity applicant waited more than three years before asking for a declaration of invalidity of the intervener’s CTM.  

The invalidity applicant failed to establish, or even allege, that prior to the filing of the mark there was any direct or indirect relationship between the parties which might explain any bad faith on the part of the intervener (paragraph 85).  

The proposal for financial compensation in the amount of USD 5 million for the transfer of the CTM, whilst considerable, does not by itself establish bad faith on the part of the intervener at the time the CTM was filed.

The fact that the signs are identical does not establish bad faith on the part of the intervener, where there are no other factors.

1. The appeal is exclusively aimed at the annulment of the contested decision insofar as the OD erred when it considered that no POU of the earlier mark could be requested from the opponent. 2. The Board notes that since the earlier French mark on which the opposition is based had not been registered for, at least, five years, on the date of publication of the CTM application, there was no obligation on the side of the opponent to provide evidence of use made of the mark. 3. The fact that the opponent might have repeatedly filed identical national applications is irrelevant to the proceedings. 4. Moreover, as regards the applicants remarks that according to the (Cancellation) Guidelines of the OHIM, applications filed repeatedly in order to avoid the obligation to prove use of the earlier mark in opposition procedures are considered to be applications filed in bad faith, the Board notes that such an example is referred restrictively to cancellation procedures relating to CTM registrations and in no case to opposition procedures. 5. The alleged opponents bad faith in filing the national French trade mark application invoked as a basis for the opposition, is an argument that cannot be assessed by the Board within the scope of opposition proceedings because bad faith as such does not constitute a ground for opposition, but may only constitute an absolute ground for a declaration of invalidity under Article 51(1)(b), furthermore, applicable only to Community trade mark registrations. 6. As regards the assessment of LOC, the Board refers to the reasons given in the contested decision, in particular, with respect to the conclusion of LOC due to the fact that the conflicting signs are highly similar overall and the contested goods in Class 9 are identical. 7. The opposition was therefore well founded on the basis of the earlier French trade mark registration invoked. 8. The appeal is dismissed.

1. The appeal is exclusively aimed at the annulment of the contested decision insofar as the OD erred when it considered that no POU of the earlier mark could be requested from the opponent. 2. The Board notes that since the earlier French mark on which the opposition is based had not been registered for, at least, five years, on the date of publication of the CTM application, there was no obligation on the side of the opponent to provide evidence of use made of the mark. 3. The fact that the opponent might have repeatedly filed identical national applications is irrelevant to the proceedings. 4. Moreover, as regards the applicants remarks that according to the (Cancellation) Guidelines of the OHIM, applications filed repeatedly in order to avoid the obligation to prove use of the earlier mark in opposition procedures are considered to be applications filed in bad faith, the Board notes that such an example is referred restrictively to cancellation procedures relating to CTM registrations and in no case to opposition procedures. 5. The alleged opponents bad faith in filing the national French trade mark application invoked as a basis for the opposition, is an argument that cannot be assessed by the Board within the scope of opposition proceedings because bad faith as such does not constitute a ground for opposition, but may only constitute an absolute ground for a declaration of invalidity under Article 51(1)(b), furthermore, applicable only to Community trade mark registrations. 6. As regards the assessment of LOC, the Board refers to the reasons given in the contested decision, in particular, with respect to the conclusion of LOC due to the fact that the conflicting signs are highly similar overall and the contested goods in Class 9 are identical. 7. The opposition was therefore well founded on the basis of the earlier French trade mark registration invoked. 8. The appeal is dismissed.

1.	When fixing the date of revocation, it is,  necessary to bear in mind that, for the purposes of Article 15 CTMR, there is no requirement that, in order to prevent revocation, a trade mark proprietor must use a trade mark during the first five years following the date of registration. Accordingly, in the present case, non-use of the contested trade mark during the period from 3 October 2000, the date on which the trade mark was registered, to 3 October 2005 cannot give rise to revocation, with the result that it is only with effect from 4 October 2005 that non-use may lead to revocation of the trade mark. Consequently, the contested decision is annulled and the date of revocation of the contested trade mark is fixed as 4 October 2005.2.	The Board annuls the contested decision insofar as it fixes the date of the application for revocation as the date on which revocation takes effect and fixes the date on which revocation takes effect as 4 October 2005.

1.	Even were the Board to consider the ‘infotrafic component to be devoid of distinctive character (at least in respect of certain services), it could not ignore the figurative components of the complex mark in question. In particular, the Board notes that the lozenge and the cartouche form, together, the unusual image of a key. This image relies heavily - even exclusively - on the figurative components of the trade mark, which are manifestly not insignificant in terms of the overall impression created by the sign and are thus likely to be remembered by the consumer of the goods and services in question, who has an average level of attentiveness. These figurative components have no descriptive character and cannot be devoid of distinctive character owing to the remarkable overall image they convey. For an in-depth analysis of the distinctive character of the ‘infotrafic component to potentially be of use for the applicant for a declaration of invalidity in the defence of a legal action instigated against it in France is insufficient reason to persuade the Board to become involved in a discussion that could have repercussions on the ongoing national dispute and thus increase the likelihood of divergent opinions. In any event, the non-applicability of Article 7(1)(b) to the trade mark in question may be inferred without it being necessary to embark upon an in-depth analysis of said term.2.	The applicant for a declaration of invalidity does not claim that the  sign applied for represents the common name in the trade for a specific product or service but, rather, that the owner is making incorrect use of its trade mark in which the word component is to the fore and the other components are much less prominent. First, such use does not seem to affect the validity of the trade mark as registered. Second, any use made of the trade mark does not demonstrate in any way that the sign, as registered, has become the usual name in the trade for a product or service for which it is protected. At most, such use could support the thesis whereby one component of the trade mark is devoid of distinctive character owing to its suitability for describing certain goods or services. Last, the Board does not share the opinion of the applicant for a declaration of invalidity whereby the proprietors use of its sign demonstrates at least implicit recognition by the owner of the signs lack of intrinsic distinctive character. Indeed, the use made of the trade mark after the date of filing of the CTM application cannot affect the validity thereof unless components are taken into account which, although post-dating the filing date of the application, allow conclusions to be drawn as to the situation as at that same filing date. Actually, in the case in point, the Board does not see how the subsequent use made of the trade mark could relate to the situation as at the date of filing of the application.3.	The appeal is dismissed.

results.quotation: (02/06/2010, R 0210/2009‑1, WUKO WORLD UNION OF KARATE-DO ORGANIZATIONS (figurative mark) / WUKO (figurative mark))
results.entityName: WUKO WORLD UNION OF KARATE-DO ORGANIZATIONS
results.nickName: WUKO WORLD UNION OF KARATE-DO ORGANIZATIONS (figurative mark) / WUKO (figurative mark)
results.summary: 1.	The CTM proprietor does not dispute the fact that he was a member of the WUKO / EKU Commission from 1977 to 1988. He is a member of the amateur association with the name of the World Union of Karate-do Organization, an organization different from that of the cancellation applicant, the purposes of which are to promote and regulate karate at an educational, ethical and sports level. The CTM proprietor is therefore a member of an association in the karate field which uses the same acronym and name as the ones which the cancellation applicant has used and which it continues to be known by. The CTM proprietor has advanced no proof that ‘WUKO is used by other organizations. In addition, both the CTM and the cancellation applicants sign are circular, with the wording ‘WUKO in a central position and around which appears a five pointed flower like figurative element. There is a strong resemblance between the conflicting signs. Consequently, it results from the forgoing that the cancellation applicant has met the onus of showing that in filing the application for the CTM, without the cancellation applicants consent, the CTM proprietor dishonestly sought to lay its hands on its ‘WUKO sign in a manner that any reasonably commercially minded person would consider falls short of standards of acceptable commercial behaviour. Thus, the CTM proprietor acted in bad faith in filing the CTM application. Accordingly, the CTM is declared invalid.2.	The appeal is dismissed.
